heroOverview: Berylliosis is an occupational granulomatous lung disease caused by an immune-mediated reaction to inhaled beryllium particles or dust. Unlike most pneumoconioses, it is not simply a dose-response injury: a genetically susceptible minority — estimated at 2-6% of those exposed — develop beryllium sensitisation, and roughly half of sensitised individuals eventually progress to chronic beryllium disease (CBD) with debilitating lung inflammation. The condition is indistinguishable clinically from pulmonary sarcoidosis until beryllium lymphocyte proliferation testing identifies the specific driver. Industries at risk include nuclear weapons manufacturing, aerospace, electronics, and dental prosthetics. Without removal from exposure and, in severe cases, treatment with immunosuppressants, progressive lung fibrosis and respiratory failure can follow.

keyStats.prevalence: Sensitisation rates of 2-10% among exposed workers; approximately 1-3% develop chronic beryllium disease (CBD) — exact U.S. figures exceed 1,000 confirmed CBD cases reported to surveillance programmes annually
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly working-age adults (20-65) in beryllium-using industries; roughly equal sex distribution in modern exposed workforces
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms of CBD typically emerge 10-15 years after first exposure, often presenting in the 40s-60s
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of thousands of workers worldwide remain occupationally exposed to beryllium; disease prevalence varies by industry controls

definition: Berylliosis (ICD-10: J63.2), also called chronic beryllium disease (CBD), is an occupational interstitial lung disease caused by a T-lymphocyte–mediated hypersensitivity reaction to beryllium, a lightweight metallic element used in high-technology alloys. Two phases are recognised: beryllium sensitisation, in which the immune system forms memory T-cells against beryllium without overt inflammation, and CBD, in which ongoing antigen stimulation drives non-caseating granuloma formation in the lungs and, less often, in lymph nodes, skin, and liver. The condition falls under pulmonology and occupational medicine. The hallmark diagnostic test is the beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT), which detects sensitised T-cells in blood or bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. Genetic susceptibility is a critical determinant: the HLA-DP2 allele (HLA-DPB1*02:01) confers a five- to twentyfold higher risk of sensitisation compared with non-carriers, explaining why most heavily exposed workers never develop disease while a susceptible minority do. The latency from first exposure to CBD symptoms averages 10-15 years but can be as short as months with intense exposure. Beryllium's unique capacity to act as a hapten — coupling with self-proteins to become immunogenic — distinguishes CBD mechanistically from the predominantly fibrogenic pneumoconioses such as silicosis and asbestosis.

typesClassification.description: Positive BeLPT in blood or BAL without clinical symptoms or radiological abnormality; indicates immune priming and approximately 6-8% annual risk of progressing to CBD.
Confirmed sensitisation with biopsy-proven non-caseating granulomas in the lung; the main form causing progressive dyspnoea, cough, and eventual fibrosis.
A rare, fulminant chemical pneumonitis caused by very high-level beryllium oxide exposure, characterised by rapid-onset pulmonary oedema and high mortality; largely eliminated by modern controls.
Skin granulomas at wound sites contaminated with beryllium particles; conjunctivitis; rare systemic involvement of lymph nodes or liver.

primarySymptoms: Progressive exertional dyspnoea — worsening breathlessness on exertion that slowly limits daily activity over months to years as granulomas replace functional lung tissue.
Dry, persistent cough that is often worse in the morning and does not respond to standard bronchodilators.
Fatigue and generalised weakness, frequently described as the most disabling symptom, disproportionate to the degree of lung function impairment.
Chest tightness or pressure, particularly after exertion, reflecting airflow limitation and reduced lung compliance.
Unintentional weight loss of more than 5% body weight over six months, seen in moderate-to-advanced disease.
Night sweats and low-grade fever, resembling the constitutional features of sarcoidosis.
Arthralgia — joint aches and stiffness, particularly in the fingers and larger joints, occurring in up to 30% of CBD patients.
Skin granulomas or persistent skin nodules at sites of previous beryllium-contaminated wound entry.
Clubbing of the fingers — enlargement of fingertip pads — in advanced disease with significant fibrosis.
Hypoxaemia at rest or on exertion, detected by pulse oximetry, indicating severe gas exchange impairment.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild breathlessness on hills or stairs that the patient attributes to deconditioning.
A dry cough that has lasted more than four weeks without an identified cause.
Abnormal chest X-ray with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy or fine nodular shadowing in an exposed worker.
A positive blood BeLPT on routine workplace surveillance, even in the absence of symptoms.
emergencySigns: Acute severe breathlessness with oxygen saturation below 90% at rest.
Acute berylliosis presentation: rapid-onset pulmonary oedema with frothy sputum after intense exposure.
Signs of cor pulmonale — ankle oedema, raised jugular venous pressure, and worsening breathlessness — indicating right heart failure from pulmonary hypertension.
Marked weight loss with haemoptysis, requiring urgent exclusion of lung cancer in this high-risk population.

causes.cause: Inhalation of beryllium-containing dust or fumes
causes.description: Beryllium particles released during machining, grinding, polishing, or sintering of beryllium metal and its alloys are inhaled and deposited in the alveoli; particle size below 10 micrometres reaches the lower airways where sensitisation begins.

causes.cause: Beryllium as a hapten triggering T-cell sensitisation
causes.description: Beryllium binds to HLA-DP molecules on antigen-presenting cells and activates CD4+ T-lymphocytes; subsequent re-exposure recruits these sensitised T-cells to the lung, triggering the granulomatous inflammation that defines CBD.

This specific HLA allele creates a binding pocket that preferentially presents beryllium-altered self-peptides, conferring a five- to twentyfold higher risk of sensitisation; the allele is present in roughly 30-35% of the general population.

Beryllium particles entering the skin through cuts or abrasions bypass respiratory defences and can sensitise the immune system via dermal antigen-presenting cells, sometimes causing local granulomas.

Family members of workers who carry beryllium home on clothes and skin, and residents near processing facilities, have developed sensitisation and CBD, demonstrating that even low-level non-workplace exposure is sufficient.

BeO ceramic components used in electronics and nuclear applications release respirable beryllium during machining or cutting; beryllium oxide is considered among the most potent sensitisers because of its fine particle formation.

riskFactors.description: Beryllium processing, nuclear weapons and reactor manufacturing, aerospace alloy fabrication, electronics, ceramics, and dental prosthetics work all involve meaningful exposure; risk rises with cumulative airborne concentration.
The single strongest known determinant; carriers have five to twentyfold higher sensitisation risk, explaining why most heavily exposed workers remain disease-free.

Though sensitisation can occur at very low levels, sustained high airborne concentrations exceed even normal immune tolerance; adequate ventilation and respiratory protection lower risk substantially.
Absence of local exhaust ventilation, open handling of beryllium powder, and failure to use supplied-air respirators dramatically increase exposure and disease risk.

riskFactors.factor: Skin injury in a beryllium environment
riskFactors.description: Cuts and abrasions occurring in beryllium dust environments allow dermal sensitisation; appropriate wound management and protective gloves reduce this route.
Smoking impairs mucociliary clearance and alters alveolar macrophage function, potentially increasing the fraction of inhaled particles reaching sensitisation-prone regions of the lung.

riskFactors.factor: Duration of employment in a beryllium industry
riskFactors.description: Workers with more than 10 years of exposure have a higher cumulative probability of sensitisation progression to CBD, though acute onset has occurred after brief intense exposure.

affectedDemographics: Workers in beryllium alloy machining, nuclear fuel processing, and aerospace manufacturing
Electronics and semiconductor industry employees handling beryllium copper components
Dental technicians working with beryllium-containing porcelain alloys
HLA-DPB1*02:01 carriers in any beryllium-exposed workforce
Household contacts of beryllium workers through take-home dust exposure
Residents living in close proximity to beryllium processing facilities
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of CBD rests on three pillars: a history of beryllium exposure, evidence of beryllium sensitisation via the beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT), and lung biopsy demonstrating non-caseating granulomas. The BeLPT is performed on peripheral blood or bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) cells, measuring T-cell proliferation in response to beryllium sulphate; a single abnormal blood BeLPT result triggers repeat testing and referral. High-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest identifies bilateral hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy, ground-glass opacification, perilymphatic nodules, and, in advanced disease, honeycombing or fibrotic distortion — a pattern closely resembling pulmonary sarcoidosis. Pulmonary function tests typically show a restrictive pattern (reduced FVC and TLC) or a mixed restrictive-obstructive pattern; the diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO) is often the most sensitive index of early disease. Fibre-optic bronchoscopy with BAL and transbronchial biopsy is performed to obtain tissue and lavage cells; a positive BAL BeLPT with compatible granulomas is diagnostic. The critical differential is pulmonary sarcoidosis, which is histologically identical; the BeLPT and occupational history are the only reliable distinguishing features. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, silicosis, and other occupational lung diseases also enter the differential.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects beryllium-specific sensitised T-cells in blood or BAL; the key test that distinguishes CBD from sarcoidosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; blood cells are cultured with beryllium and the proliferation response measured over several days. Abnormal results are confirmed on a repeat sample.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies lymphadenopathy, ground-glass change, nodules, and fibrosis to stage the extent of disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a CT scanner for a few minutes; no contrast injection is needed for standard chest HRCT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies restriction, obstruction, and impaired gas transfer to establish disease severity and track progression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe through a mouthpiece in a body box or spirometer; the test is effort-dependent and takes 30-60 minutes total.

diagnosticTests.test: Bronchoscopy with BAL and transbronchial biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains BAL cells for BAL BeLPT and tissue for histology to confirm granulomas and exclude other diagnoses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under moderate sedation; a thin flexible scope is passed through the nose or mouth into the airways; you may cough for a day or two afterward.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses functional exercise capacity and detects exertional oxygen desaturation, which guides supplemental oxygen decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You walk as far as you can in six minutes along a flat corridor while a physiotherapist monitors your oxygen levels and heart rate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to screen for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and parenchymal infiltrates in exposed workers; less sensitive than HRCT.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard, painless two-minute chest X-ray; results are read by a radiologist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment aims to suppress the granulomatous inflammation, preserve lung function, and manage symptoms. Removal from further beryllium exposure is the first essential step for all sensitised workers and CBD patients, though it does not reverse established disease. For symptomatic CBD or disease showing progressive decline in pulmonary function, systemic corticosteroids — typically oral prednisolone at 0.5 mg/kg/day — are the mainstay of treatment and can stabilise or modestly improve lung function in the majority of patients. Because long-term corticosteroids carry significant side effects including osteoporosis, diabetes, and immunosuppression, the dose is tapered to the lowest effective level once disease stabilises, usually over 6-12 months. Steroid-sparing agents such as methotrexate or azathioprine are added when steroid doses cannot be adequately reduced or when disease progresses despite steroids. Supplemental oxygen is prescribed when resting oxygen saturation falls below 88% or desaturation occurs on exertion. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance and quality of life even in advanced disease. Lung transplantation is an option for end-stage CBD with acceptable candidacy; outcomes are similar to transplant for other interstitial lung diseases. Vaccinations against influenza and pneumococcus are recommended for all patients on immunosuppressants. Workplace surveillance programmes using BeLPT screening allow early identification of sensitised workers, enabling removal before CBD develops.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: The primary treatment for symptomatic CBD; suppresses T-cell-driven granulomatous inflammation. Started at 0.5 mg/kg/day and tapered over months to the lowest dose controlling symptoms. Long-term use requires bone protection and monitoring for steroid complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilises pulmonary function in approximately 60-70% of treated patients; frank improvement in FVC or DLCO occurs in roughly 30-40%.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate
medicalTreatments.description: A steroid-sparing agent used when prednisolone cannot be tapered without disease progression or when side effects are limiting; typically given weekly as a low-dose tablet or injection with folic acid supplementation and regular liver function monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Allows successful prednisolone reduction in approximately 50-60% of steroid-dependent CBD patients in case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative steroid-sparing immunosuppressant for CBD patients intolerant of methotrexate; requires thiopurine methyltransferase testing before use and monitoring for bone marrow suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates comparable to methotrexate in small CBD series, though head-to-head evidence is limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribed when resting SpO2 is consistently at or below 88% or when exertional desaturation limits activity; improves exercise tolerance and reduces strain on the right heart.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term oxygen therapy reduces mortality and improves quality of life in hypoxaemic interstitial lung disease patients, with benefit supported by data from ILD trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: A supervised programme of aerobic and strength exercise, breathing techniques, and education; does not alter disease biology but consistently improves six-minute walk distance and breathlessness scores.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves six-minute walk distance by an average of 40-50 metres and reduces dyspnoea scores by clinically meaningful amounts in ILD including CBD.

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Considered for patients with end-stage CBD, declining lung function despite maximal therapy, and acceptable candidacy without major contraindications; beryllium-specific immune activation can potentially recur in the allograft.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year survival after transplantation for interstitial lung disease is approximately 50-55%, similar to outcomes for pulmonary fibrosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Exercise training, education, and psychological support supervised by physiotherapists; improves functional capacity and quality of life without altering underlying disease; strongly recommended for all CBD patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Workplace removal and exposure reduction
alternativeTreatments.description: Though not a medical treatment, immediate removal from beryllium exposure upon sensitisation diagnosis is the single most important secondary prevention step; it slows but does not halt progression once CBD is established.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist with experience in interstitial lung diseases and occupational lung disorders should evaluate all patients with suspected beryllium sensitisation or CBD. Accurate BeLPT interpretation, bronchoscopy, and staging require specialist input. An occupational medicine physician is essential to assess workplace exposure history, facilitate removal from exposure, and support employers with hazard control. Regular specialist monitoring is needed because progression can occur even after exposure ends.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a pulmonologist with specific experience in occupational lung diseases or interstitial lung disease clinics. Academic medical centres with occupational medicine departments are best equipped to perform BeLPT testing, interpret bronchoscopy findings, and manage immunosuppressant regimens. Ask whether the team has experience distinguishing CBD from sarcoidosis and managing long-term steroid-sparing protocols.
preventionStrategies: Implement engineering controls — local exhaust ventilation, enclosed machining, wet methods — to keep airborne beryllium below 0.2 micrograms per cubic metre (OSHA action level).
Use supplied-air respirators or tight-fitting NIOSH-certified respirators when engineering controls alone cannot maintain safe levels.
Establish and maintain a medical surveillance programme with BeLPT screening for all exposed workers, enabling detection of sensitisation before CBD develops.
Counsel HLA-DPB1*02:01 carriers about their elevated personal risk; some choose voluntary reassignment from beryllium tasks.
Prevent take-home contamination by providing workplace showering and changing facilities and prohibiting beryllium-contaminated clothing from leaving the site.
Manage skin wounds occurring in beryllium environments promptly and document exposures.
lifestyleModifications: Stop all tobacco use; smoking impairs pulmonary defence mechanisms and accelerates lung function decline.
Eat a calcium and vitamin D-rich diet and take supplementation as prescribed if on long-term corticosteroids.
Monitor body weight monthly; both steroid-related weight gain and disease-related weight loss require attention.
Pace activities to conserve energy on bad-symptom days; an occupational therapist can help redesign daily routines.
Avoid respiratory infections by practising good hand hygiene and staying current with vaccinations.
Attend pulmonary rehabilitation regularly to maintain as much exercise capacity as possible.

recommended: Calcium-rich foods — dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens — to counteract corticosteroid-induced bone loss
Vitamin D-rich foods — oily fish, eggs, fortified foods — alongside any prescribed supplementation
Adequate protein intake to prevent muscle wasting on long-term corticosteroids
Small, frequent meals if breathlessness limits the ability to eat large portions
avoid: Excess salt and processed foods, which raise blood pressure — a common steroid side effect
Concentrated sugary foods, particularly on corticosteroids, which raise blood glucose
Alcohol above low-risk limits when on methotrexate, as the combination is hepatotoxic

exerciseGuidelines: Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation is strongly encouraged for all CBD patients with functional limitation. Aerobic activities such as walking, cycling, and swimming at a moderate intensity are appropriate; pace to symptoms and use supplemental oxygen during exercise if prescribed. Avoid outdoor exercise during dust storms, high-pollution days, or in environments where beryllium exposure could recur. Strength training helps counter corticosteroid-related muscle loss.
dailyManagement: Take immunosuppressant medication at the same time each day and do not self-adjust doses without specialist advice.
Monitor oxygen saturation with a home pulse oximeter before, during, and after exertion if exertional desaturation has been documented.
Record any new or worsening symptoms — increasing breathlessness, chest pain, or haemoptysis — and report them promptly to your specialist.
Carry an emergency action plan detailing when to seek urgent medical review.
Ensure annual influenza vaccination and up-to-date pneumococcal vaccination.
prognosis: Prognosis in CBD is variable and strongly influenced by the degree of functional impairment at diagnosis and the rate of disease progression. Approximately half of patients show gradual progressive decline in pulmonary function despite treatment, while a substantial minority stabilise with corticosteroids and remain at an acceptable quality of life for many years. Those diagnosed at the sensitisation stage — before CBD develops — can avoid progression simply by removing themselves from exposure. Advanced disease with significant fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension, or marked reduction in DLCO carries a meaningfully increased mortality; median survival from advanced CBD presenting with severe restriction is estimated at 8-12 years in older cohorts, though outcomes improve with earlier detection. Lung cancer risk is elevated in this population, as beryllium is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC).
recoveryTimeline: There is no cure; the timeline for CBD is one of management rather than recovery. Within 3-6 months of starting corticosteroids, most patients notice improvement in breathlessness and fatigue. Pulmonary function stabilisation, if it occurs, is usually apparent by 12 months. Patients whose disease continues to decline despite steroids are transitioned to steroid-sparing agents over a 6-12 month period. Long-term follow-up is lifelong, with specialist review every 6-12 months including spirometry and DLCO.
complications: Progressive pulmonary fibrosis — granulomas are replaced by scar tissue over years, causing irreversible restriction and gas-exchange failure.
Pulmonary hypertension — raised pressure in the pulmonary arteries from hypoxia and fibrosis, leading to right heart failure if untreated.
Lung cancer — beryllium is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen; CBD patients have an approximately twofold elevated lung cancer risk compared with unexposed controls.
Osteoporosis and vertebral fracture — a consequence of long-term corticosteroid therapy; requires bone protection from the outset.
Corticosteroid-related diabetes mellitus — hyperglycaemia occurs in up to 20% of patients on maintenance prednisolone and requires monitoring and management.
Respiratory failure — end-stage CBD can progress to type 1 respiratory failure requiring long-term oxygen therapy or lung transplant assessment.

supportResources.name: NIOSH — Beryllium
supportResources.description: Occupational exposure guidance, health effects, and workplace controls from the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health.

supportResources.name: OSHA — Beryllium Standard
supportResources.description: U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration standard for beryllium exposure in general industry, construction, and shipyards.

supportResources.name: American Thoracic Society — Occupational Lung Disease
supportResources.description: Patient education on occupational lung disease from a leading respiratory medicine professional society.

sources.title: American Thoracic Society Statement on Diagnosis and Management of Beryllium Sensitivity and Chronic Beryllium Disease. Am J Respir Crit Care Med, 2014
Mroz MM et al. Chronic Beryllium Disease. Clin Chest Med, 2012
NIOSH — Beryllium: Health Effects and Exposure Standards
IARC Monographs Volume 58: Beryllium as a Group 1 Carcinogen

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause granulomatous lung disease, but TB granulomas are typically caseating (central necrosis), are caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and are diagnosed by sputum culture or nucleic acid testing — not by BeLPT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Silicosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Silicosis is a fibrogenic pneumoconiosis from silica, producing dense nodules predominantly in the upper lobes; it lacks beryllium-specific T-cell sensitisation and the BeLPT is negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute interstitial pneumonitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute interstitial pneumonitis is a rapid, diffuse lung injury of unknown trigger, without beryllium exposure history or granulomas, and responds differently to treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Silicosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another occupational granulomatous/fibrotic lung disease caused by inhaled mineral dust, managed similarly with removal from exposure and pulmonary support.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: An occupational interstitial lung disease sharing progressive fibrosis and malignancy risk; asbestos causes fibrosis by a different mechanism but the clinical management overlaps.

relatedConditions.name: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of the same group of occupational dust lung diseases; contrasted with CBD to distinguish dose-response fibrosis from immune-mediated granulomatosis.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another work-related immune lung disease; differentiated from CBD by its airway focus and reversible airflow obstruction rather than parenchymal granulomas.

faqs.question: What is berylliosis?
faqs.answer: Berylliosis, also called chronic beryllium disease (CBD), is a lung condition caused by an immune reaction to inhaled beryllium dust or fumes in the workplace. It causes granulomas — tiny clusters of inflammatory cells — to form in the lungs, leading to breathlessness, cough, and fatigue. It resembles sarcoidosis but is specifically triggered by beryllium.

faqs.question: Who gets berylliosis?
faqs.answer: Berylliosis affects workers exposed to beryllium in industries such as aerospace, nuclear weapons manufacturing, electronics, and dental prosthetics. Only 2-10% of exposed workers develop sensitisation, and only about half of those progress to chronic beryllium disease. The HLA-DPB1*02:01 gene markedly raises an individual's risk.

faqs.question: Is berylliosis the same as sarcoidosis?
faqs.answer: Not exactly. Berylliosis and sarcoidosis are histologically identical — both cause non-caseating granulomas in the lungs — and they look the same on scans. The key difference is cause: berylliosis is driven by a specific immune response to beryllium and is diagnosed by the beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT), which is negative in sarcoidosis.

faqs.question: How is chronic beryllium disease diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires three things: a history of beryllium exposure, a positive beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT) confirming immune sensitisation, and a lung biopsy showing non-caseating granulomas. A CT scan and pulmonary function tests assess the extent and severity. Sarcoidosis, which looks identical, is excluded by the BeLPT and occupational history.

faqs.question: Is berylliosis curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for established chronic beryllium disease. Removing yourself from beryllium exposure slows progression and is essential. Corticosteroids can suppress inflammation and stabilise lung function in many patients, but granulomas and any fibrosis that has developed do not disappear. Early detection at the sensitisation stage, before CBD develops, offers the best chance of preventing permanent damage.

faqs.question: What is the beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test?
faqs.answer: The BeLPT measures whether your immune cells (T-lymphocytes) react abnormally to beryllium sulphate in the laboratory. A positive result means your immune system has been sensitised to beryllium. The test is performed on a standard blood sample or on cells washed from the airways during bronchoscopy. It is the only test that reliably distinguishes chronic beryllium disease from sarcoidosis.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of berylliosis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms of chronic beryllium disease are progressive breathlessness on exertion, a dry persistent cough, fatigue, chest tightness, and unintentional weight loss. Joint aches, night sweats, and low-grade fever also occur. Symptoms usually appear 10-15 years after first exposure and worsen gradually over years.

faqs.question: Can berylliosis progress after stopping exposure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic beryllium disease can continue to progress even after complete removal from beryllium exposure because the immune reaction is self-sustaining once established. Stopping exposure is still essential — it removes the stimulus driving new sensitisation and slows progression — but established CBD requires monitoring and often ongoing immunosuppressive treatment.

faqs.question: How is berylliosis treated?
faqs.answer: The first step is removing the patient from any further beryllium exposure. For symptomatic CBD or disease that is progressing, oral corticosteroids such as prednisolone are the primary treatment and stabilise lung function in about 60-70% of patients. When steroids cannot be tapered without relapse, methotrexate or azathioprine are added. Severe cases may be considered for lung transplantation.

faqs.question: Does berylliosis increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Beryllium is classified as a Group 1 human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). Workers with chronic beryllium disease have approximately twice the lung cancer risk of unexposed individuals. Regular chest imaging as part of ongoing medical surveillance helps detect any new lung cancer at an early stage.

faqs.question: Is berylliosis an occupational disease?
faqs.answer: Berylliosis is almost exclusively an occupational disease, caused by workplace exposure to beryllium dust or fumes. In most countries it is a notifiable occupational disease, qualifying affected workers for workers' compensation. Rare cases occur from neighbourhood exposure to beryllium processing facilities or from take-home dust on a worker's clothing.

faqs.question: What industries are at risk for berylliosis?
faqs.answer: The highest-risk industries are nuclear weapons and reactor manufacturing, aerospace alloy fabrication, beryllium metal processing, electronics and semiconductor production (beryllium copper contacts), and dental prosthetics using beryllium-containing porcelain alloys. Secondary risk exists for maintenance, ventilation, and cleaning workers at these facilities.

faqs.question: Can family members of beryllium workers get berylliosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cases of beryllium sensitisation and chronic beryllium disease have occurred in household contacts who had no direct workplace exposure, caused by beryllium dust carried home on workers' clothes, hair, and skin. This is why workplaces are required to provide showering facilities and prevent contaminated clothing from leaving the site.

faqs.question: What is the difference between beryllium sensitisation and chronic beryllium disease?
faqs.answer: Beryllium sensitisation means your immune system has reacted to beryllium — your BeLPT is positive — but you have no symptoms or lung damage yet. Chronic beryllium disease means the immune reaction has caused active granulomatous inflammation in the lung with symptoms and measurable lung impairment. About half of sensitised workers develop CBD, often years later.

faqs.question: How is berylliosis different from silicosis?
faqs.answer: Silicosis is caused by inhaling crystalline silica and involves a predominantly fibrogenic (scarring) reaction without specific immune sensitisation. Berylliosis is caused by beryllium and involves a T-lymphocyte immune response, making it more closely related to sarcoidosis than to silicosis. The BeLPT distinguishes them, and silicosis produces different, denser X-ray nodules mainly in the upper lobes.

faqs.question: What long-term monitoring is needed for berylliosis?
faqs.answer: All sensitised workers and CBD patients need lifelong follow-up. This typically includes spirometry and DLCO every 6-12 months, chest imaging (CT or X-ray), blood tests if on immunosuppressants, and BeLPT surveillance. Bone density scanning is advised for patients on long-term steroids. Any new symptoms, particularly haemoptysis or rapidly worsening breathlessness, need prompt reassessment.

faqs.question: What prevents berylliosis in the workplace?
faqs.answer: Prevention centres on keeping airborne beryllium below safe limits through local exhaust ventilation and enclosed processes, using respirators when engineering controls are insufficient, conducting medical surveillance with BeLPT testing, and educating workers. The U.S. OSHA beryllium standard sets an action level of 0.1 micrograms per cubic metre and a permissible exposure limit of 0.2 micrograms per cubic metre.

faqs.question: Does berylliosis affect organs other than the lungs?
faqs.answer: The lungs are the primary target, but berylliosis can also affect the skin (granulomas at wound sites), lymph nodes, liver, spleen, and kidneys in a minority of patients. Skin and eye involvement tends to occur in those with direct skin contact with beryllium. Systemic involvement is less common than in sarcoidosis but does occur in advanced disease.

faqs.question: Can you work after a berylliosis diagnosis?
faqs.answer: People with berylliosis must stop all work involving beryllium exposure immediately after diagnosis. Many can continue working in other roles without beryllium contact, depending on their functional capacity. Significant respiratory impairment may limit ability to perform physically demanding work. An occupational medicine physician can advise on workplace accommodations and workers' compensation entitlements.

faqs.question: How quickly does berylliosis progress?
faqs.answer: Progression varies widely. Some patients stabilise with treatment and have only mild limitation for decades. Others show gradual decline in lung function over 5-10 years despite therapy. A minority progress rapidly to respiratory failure. Disease caught at the sensitisation stage, before CBD develops, carries the best prognosis, which is why surveillance programmes that identify workers early are so important.

metaDescription: Berylliosis is an immune-mediated occupational lung disease from beryllium exposure causing granulomas. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment options.
